titleInfo.title: The Scripture on the Ten Kings and the Making of Purgatory in Medieval Chinese Buddhism

mods.abstract: p. 98: S 5450, S 5544 (dated 911?), and san 262: "... stating that the text is based on "a true printed copy of the Kuo family of Hsi-ch’uan" (Hsi-ch’uan Kuo-chia chen yin-pen.)" 西川過家真印本 There are more examples of Diamondsutras with this note. Teiser notes p. 98-99: "The printed edition belonging originally to the Kuo family, then, must have been revered so highly in at least one Tun-huang scriptorium that it served as the master version for the production of many copies." CW: Does 真印本 really refer to the master version, in terms of the correct or verified version? Is this printing technology? I don’t think so. I assume 真印 here has a religious meaning. This master version was copied many times comparable to the true images of the Buddha. It is not a "true printed copy", but rather the "text of the true seal", ultimately referring to an efficacious text. CW 2024: 真印本 refers to the "true" Kumarajiva version of the Diamond, the one with the additional 60/62 characters that were believed to have been long lost. p. 196-219: translation of The Scripture of King Yama Rāja Concerning the Sevens of Life to Be Cultivated in Preparation for Rebirth in the Pure Land 閻羅王預修生七往生净土經 on the basis of a critical edition of the long recension derived from six tenth-century manuscripts: P.2003; P.2870; Satō ms; S.3961; P.3761; Cft 00212 (L).
